coredata.dc:title: Functionalization of graphene and few-layer graphene with aqueous solution of hydrofluoric acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Nebogatikova, N.A.
Antonova, I.V.
Volodin, V.A.
Prinz, V.Ya.
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, conditions suitable for efficient modification of graphene and few-layer graphene (FLG) films with aqueous solutions of hydrofluoric acid (HF) and for local protection of the graphene against such modification in isopropyl alcohol were identified. A combination of the two treatments gives one a key to nanodesign of graphene-based 2D devices. It was found that a few-minute treatment of graphene or FLG in HF aqueous solutions (∼1min for graphene and ∼5min for FLG films about 5nm thick) leads to strong changes in the structural and electrical properties of graphene involving a step-like increase in resistivity (up to 1011 Ω/□). Two types of materials were obtained after different times of treatment: (i) promising for electronic applications of the material due to a combination of high carrier mobility, high conductivity, and strong current modulation by gate voltage (up to four orders of magnitude); (ii) a material with insulating properties and graphene quantum dots embedded in an insulating matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene functionalization
Hydrofluoric acid
Transport properties
Current modulation
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrical properties of graphene functionalized with HF
Interaction of HF and IPA with graphene

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was partially supported by the Russian Foundation for Basic Research (grants nos. 12–02–01275 and 11–02–00722 ) and by the Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation (project no. 8028 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Raman spectrum of a 5-nm thick FLG flake treated with 5% solution of HF in water for different times. The inset shows the position of the excitation laser beam on one of the graphene flakes.

AFM images of the surface of a pristine (non-treated) graphene flake (a) and the surface of graphene samples treated in 5% solution of HF in water for different times, 4, 7, and 11min (respectively, b, c, and d).

(a) Current–voltage characteristics of FLG flakes treated in 5% solution of HF in water for different times and (b) the resistivity of various flakes of thicknesses 3 to 6nm versus HF-treatment duration t. The FLG thickness is indicated in the figure as a parameter. The curve labeled IPA in (b) refers to a sample that preliminarily (before the treatment in HF:H2O) was treated for 20min in isopropyl alcohol (IPA). Inset in (b) shows the current-voltage characteristic of a sample with 5-nm thick FLG treated in HF:H2O for 5min. (c) Flake resistivity of samples with strongly different FLG thicknesses as a function of HF-treatment duration. (d) Resistivity of a 4-nm thick FLG flake treated in HF:H2O (treatment duration is 8min) versus isochronal annealing temperature. The annealing time was 30min.

Current–voltage characteristics of an FLG flake treated in 5% solution of HF in water for 4min. The characteristics were measured at temperatures 80 and 300K in transistor mode using the substrate as the gate electrode. The gate voltage V ds was 200mV, and the flake thickness, 3nm. The inset shows data simulated using Eq. (1).

head.ce:title: Functionalization of graphene and few-layer graphene with aqueous solution of hydrofluoric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: A V Rzhanov Institute of Semiconductor Physics SB RAS, Novosibirsk, 13 Lavrentiev avenue, 630090, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: A V Rzhanov Institute of Semiconductor Physics SB RAS

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Novosibirsk State University, Novosibirsk, 2 Pirogov street, 630090, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, conditions suitable for efficient modification of graphene and few-layer graphene (FLG) films with aqueous solutions of hydrofluoric acid (HF) and for local protection of the graphene against such modification in isopropyl alcohol were identified. A combination of the two treatments gives one a key to nanodesign of graphene-based 2D devices. It was found that a few-minute treatment of graphene or FLG in HF aqueous solutions (∼1min for graphene and ∼5min for FLG films about 5nm thick) leads to strong changes in the structural and electrical properties of graphene involving a step-like increase in resistivity (up to 1011 Ω/□). Two types of materials were obtained after different times of treatment: (i) promising for electronic applications of the material due to a combination of high carrier mobility, high conductivity, and strong current modulation by gate voltage (up to four orders of magnitude); (ii) a material with insulating properties and graphene quantum dots embedded in an insulating matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Extraordinary properties of graphene continue attracting much attention of researchers, this research having evolved rapidly in recent years [1,2]. One of the main lines along which progress in graphene-based technologies was achieved is chemical functionalization of graphene or few-layer graphene (FLG) with various substances, with chemical reactions of graphene layers with atoms or molecules causing a pronounced change in the properties of the material [3,4]. Today, the following main derivatives of graphene attracting huge interest are known: (i) fluorographene (FG), a stoichiometric derivative of graphene with fluorine atoms attached to carbon atoms [5]; and (ii) graphane, a stoichiometric derivative of graphene with hydrogen atoms attached to carbon atoms [6]. Graphane was found to be a substance low-stable at room and moderately elevated temperatures, T ∼200–300°C, because of the escape of hydrogen atoms from the material [6,7]. Unlike graphane, fluorographene is stable up to temperature 400°C, this material being a high-quality insulator (resistivity >1012 Ω/□) with about 3-eV optical bandgap [5,8]. These properties in combination with interesting mechanical properties [5,8] make FG a very promising material for obtaining 2D insulators and for other important applications.

Synthesis of FG is generally performed using two methods. In the first method, mechanical exfoliation of graphene flakes from fluorinated graphite is used. Fluorinated graphite is normally created by exposing graphite to a fluorinating agent, F2 or XeF2, admixed to some ambient medium; this process, held at temperatures 120–400°C, normally takes more than 10h [5,8,9]. Graphite fluoride can also be obtained at room temperature using a reaction of graphite with a gaseous mixture of F2, HF, or MFn (the volatile fluorides MFn used were IF5, BF3 and ClF3) with subsequent treatment of the resulting material in a fluorine-containing ambient at temperatures 100–600°C [11]. Mechanical cleaving of individual FG atomic planes from commercially available fluorinated graphite was found to be a surprisingly difficult task: FG sheets were found to be extremely fragile and prone to rupture due to many structural defects resulting from the harsh fluorination conditions used to obtain bulk fluorinated graphite [8]. In the second method, FG is created by mechanical exfoliation of graphene flakes from graphite with subsequent fluorination of the flakes by a procedure quite similar to that normally used to achieve graphite fluorination. Due to small sizes of graphene flakes, it is possible to obtain fully fluorinated graphene flakes by a XeF2 agent using treatments lasting for about two weeks [5]. Since XeF2 rapidly etches Si and readily diffuses even through amorphous SiO2, it appears impossible to employ SiO2/Si substrates in the fluorination procedure; using a chemically inert support is therefore required. The need for an inert support makes the whole process rather a complicated procedure.

In the present study, we propose the formation of fluorographene (and also few-layer fluorographene) via functionalization of graphene samples in aqueous solutions of hydrofluoric acid (HF:H2O). It was found that this technological treatment, a common one in silicon technology, allows one, as applied to graphene, to create functionalized graphene in a few minutes. It was demonstrated that graphene or FLG partially functionalized with HF presents promising materials for transistor applications due to a combination of high carrier mobility, high conductivity, and strong modulation of the current by gate voltage. The increase in the duration of the treatment leads to the formation of a high-quality insulator (resistivity ≥1011 Ω/□), with a nanoswell relief of about 100-nm period present on its surface. Formation of graphene quantum dots in an insulated matrix is assumed to proceed after longer HF treatments. Treatment of few-layer graphene in isopropyl alcohol given to samples prior to their treatment in HF:H2O protects the graphene against subsequent fluorination in HF:H2O. Thus, the combination of treatments in isopropyl alcohol and in an aqueous solution of hydrofluoric acid gives one a possibility to design electronic devices.

Graphene and FLG films used in the present study were prepared by electrostatic exfoliation of graphene from highly ordered pyrolytic graphite (HOPG). In this way, FLG flakes about 6nm thick and typically sized (10–30)×(50–100)µm2 were obtained on top of silicon substrates covered by 300nm of SiO2. Electrical contacts to graphene flakes in our samples were prepared before the chemical treatments using either silver or gold.

Hydrofluoric acid is widely used in silicon technology. It is known that graphene or carbon tubes treated in solutions of hydrofluoric acid in water show an enhanced stability when stressed with electric voltages in comparison with non-modified tubes. Graphene edge passivation and defect decoration in graphene are considered to be responsible for the enhanced stability [11]. Treatments in HF aqueous solutions are also widely used in fabricating suspended graphene films [12,13].

In the present study, graphene or FLG samples were subjected to treatments in 5% aqueous solutions of HF at room temperature. The treatment duration was ranged from 0.5 to 20min. In the case of electrical measurements, this duration was limited by the moment when the insulating layer of silicon dioxide was removed from the silicon surface. Generally, HF-etched oxide layer with a thickness down to 50nm provided sufficient insulation of the graphene film from the substrate.

A Solver PRO NT-MDT scanning probe microscope was used to characterize our samples. The thickness of our structures was determined by measurements performed in atomic force microscopy (AFM) mode. Current–voltage (I–V) characteristics of graphene layers were measured at temperatures 80 and 300K using a Keithley picoammeter (model 6485). The measurements were performed both in two-terminal and field-effect-transistor (FET) configurations using the substrate as the gate electrode (respectively, curves I ds(V ds) and I ds(V g)). Micro-Raman spectroscopy measurements were carried out under ambient conditions at room temperature using the 514.5-nm (2.41-eV) excitation line of an argon ion laser. The laser power shone onto the sample was restricted to 2–3mW to avoid laser-induced heating. The Raman spectra were recorded in back-scattering geometry. A triple T64000 Horiba Jobin Yvon spectrometer equipped with a micro-Raman setup was used. The spectra were registered in Z(XY)Z polarization geometry: the polarizations of incident and scattered light were perpendicular to each other.

A comparison of the structural properties of pristine FLG films and structural properties of FLG films modified in HF aqueous solutions were studied using Raman spectroscopy under ambient conditions. Fig. 1 shows changes in the Raman spectra of several samples treated in HF aqueous solution for different times up to 11min. The peak 2D is the second-order scattering band. The spectra of pristine samples and samples treated in HF:H2O for 3min were practically identical. The most prominent peak G at ∼1580cm−1 is known to be due to in-plane vibrations of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. The peak D at 1350cm−1 is an indicator of some intrinsic defects present in graphene sheets or a basal-plane chemical reaction breaking the π-bonds and converting the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms into sp3 hybridization [14,15]. The dynamics of Raman-spectrum changes includes an initial increase in D-peak intensity and a subsequent decrease of the intensity of all other peaks. The position of excitation laser spot on the samples was checked using an optical camera. One of the images taken with the optical camera is shown in the inset to Fig. 1. Changes in the Raman spectra are similar to the dynamics of the Raman spectra changes observed for fluorographene [5,8–10]. We therefore assume that treatments of our samples in the 5% solution of HF in water during times longer than 5min led to the formation of fluorographene or few-layer fluorographene films.

Dramatic changes in the Raman spectra show that graphene functionalization in HF:H2O occurs in all monolayers of the used few-layer graphene. Intercalation of HF into the FLG interlayer space during a few-minute treatment is impossible. Thus, the modification of graphene surface morphology is assumed to be due to functionalization of graphene with HF. AFM images of FLG films treated in aqueous solution of HF for different times are shown in Fig. 2b, c, d; these images are to be compared to the image of the surface of a pristine sample shown in Fig. 2a. Treatment in HF:H2O leads to the formation of an irregular surface morphology in samples treated in HF:H2O for 4min (the first stage of graphene functionalization with HF); later, this morphology transforms in a regular swell-like periodic morphology (corrugation) of the surface (the second functionalization stage). An increase in the duration of HF treatment at the second stage induces no further changes in the size and height of the corrugation. These surface transformations can be expected to also manifest themselves in the electrical properties of partially and completely fluorinated few-layer graphene.
The increase in FLG thickness over 15–20nm suppresses changes in the surface morphology of FLG films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical properties of graphene functionalized with HF
ce:section.ce:para: Formation of an insulator network, a strong abrupt increase in the conductivity of graphene structures due to the completion of this network, the presence of conductive islands in the insulating matrix, and subsequent vanishing of these islands from the graphene films are the main steps in the transformation of electrical properties of examined graphene and FLG films revealed in the present study during prolonged functionalization of the graphemes with HF. Among the above steps, the last one corresponds to treatment durations at which all peaks vanish from the Raman spectra of the samples.

The current–voltage characteristics of our samples measured in two-terminal configuration are shown in Fig. 3a. Linear I ds–V ds characteristics exhibited by all structures were indicative of an ohmic behavior of prepared contacts. The resistivity of FLG samples 3 to 6nm thick is plotted in Fig. 3b as a function of HF-treatment duration. As it follows from the plot, at the first stage of HF treatment lasting for 4–5min only weak changes in the I ds—V ds characteristics and FLG resistivity are observed. At longer durations of HF treatment, a step-like increase in FLG resistivity was revealed (transition to an insulating state). The abrupt increase in resistivity for a large set of examined samples with thicknesses 4–6nm was found to occur at treatment durations 5–6min. Such times correspond to functionalization of all monolayers in the examined structures with HF.

Fig. 3c shows the resistivity of FLG flakes of different thicknesses as a function of HF-treatment duration. An increase of resistivity in graphene (and bilayer graphene) films was observed following about 1-min treatment duration. FLG structures with FLG thickness greater than 15–20nm exhibited no increase in resistivity. The latter observation proves that the increase in resistivity is not due to the drop of voltage at the contacts. An increase in the flake thickness in excess of 15–20nm leads to vanishing of the resistivity step at least for HF treatments kept for more than 40min.

The times of occurrence of the resistivity step for all samples with different thicknesses of FLG flakes show a good correlation with the decrease of peak intensities in the Raman spectra and with the appearance of a regular swell-like periodic morphology on the surface of HF-treated samples as observed by AFM. The increase of flake thickness in excess of 15–20nm completely suppresses all manifestations of the HF-induced functionalization of graphene films: in the latter cases, no nanoswell morphology on the sample surface and no resistivity step were observed.

The properties of the HF-treated structures were found to be quite stable. This was proved by repeated measurements of electrical and structural properties of our graphene samples subjected to HF functionalization procedure.

Thermal stability and reversible behavior of our functionalized samples was tested by subjecting them to additional anneals. It is known that the hydrogen desorption from graphene occurs at temperatures ∼200–300°C [6]. On the other hand, C–F bonds are stable up to 400°C [5,8]. We have performed an anneal of one of our FG samples at temperature 300°C for 1h. As previously, after the annealing this structure exhibited the same high resistivity. Isochronal (30min) annealing in Ar ambient was found to lead to a recovery of conductivity at 450°C (see Fig. 3d). This observation provides an additional argument that HF treatment causes fluorination, and not graphene hydrogenation or oxidation.

The observed transformations of network surface morphology into a nanoswell relief during longer HF treatments allow us to suggest the presence of graphene conductive islands (quantum dots) in the functionalized insulating graphene matrix after the step-like increase of structure resistivity. This suggestion is made based on the following experimental results. It was found that, right after the step-like increase in resistivity (treatment durations ∼5min in Fig. 3b) the conductivity of samples could be increased by applying higher voltages (about 7V) to the samples. The inset in Fig. 3b shows the increase in the electric current through an HF-treated (5min) sample that was induced by a high applied voltage, and also a hysteresis demonstrated by this current during the reverse voltage swept. Moreover, the application of pulsed voltages (charge deep level transient spectroscopy) clearly demonstrates that the capture of charge carriers by, and their emission from, the conductive islands. An increase in the HF treatment duration leads to a complete vanishing of conductivity in our samples up to voltages of ±20V. The latter fact is due to the increase in the thickness of the HF-functionalized part of our structures and the decrease in the size of the graphene conductive islands, both resulting in an increased width of barriers formed around the conductive islands. The possibility of graphene quantum dot formation in a fluorinated graphene matrix was demonstrated in the theoretical study by Ribas et al. [16]. Formation of graphene quantum dots was also predicted for a corrugated graphene surface due to huge changes of the strain at nanometer scale [17]. The size of graphene quantum dots as estimated from observation of quantum confinement effects was found to be ≤100nm [18]. This size well correlates with the nanoswell relief observed in our samples (period ∼200nm).

Let us consider now in more detail results of electrical measurements that were obtained for samples treated in HF:H2O during times shorter than those necessary for the occurrence of the resistivity step (e.g. about 5min in Fig. 3 b). The I ds (V g) characteristics measured in transistor configuration at two different temperatures using the substrate as the gate electrode for an FLG flake treated in 5% solution of HF in water during 4min are shown in Fig. 4. Surprisingly, here we found that the drain-source current I ds varied over 4–5 orders of magnitude with variation of gate voltage V g. Before the functionalization in HF:H2O in this structure the current modulation by gate voltage was very weak (only few percents). Note that, for instance, in the study reported in [9], which was aimed at preparation of a field-effect transistor on fluorographene at room temperature, the current was modulated only within a factor of two at room temperature yet by two orders of magnitude at temperature 4.2K. So, our films demonstrated a high potential in management of their conductivity. The neutrality (Dirac) point in the I ds(V g) characteristics of our samples showed a shift toward positive voltages V g (0.2–0.5V) (see Fig. 4). Using the relation n = γ Δ
V
g
, where
γ
= 7
.4x10
10
cm
− 2
V
− 1
[9], we were therefore able to evaluate the hole concentration in the fluorinated material at different temperatures; this evaluation typically yielded values of 2×1010 cm−2 at 80K and 4×1010 cm−2 at 300K.

The conductivity of graphehe or FLG structures σ ds (see inset in Fig. 4) can be fitted with the formula [19].
(1)

ds

) =

] )
− 1

In (1), σ bg =σ ds(V 0) is the background conductivity at the gate voltage V g=V 0, ρ r is the resistivity due to the contacts, V ds is the source-drain voltage, and C g is the gate oxide capacitance (C g=1.15×10–8 F/cm2 for 300-nm SiO2 and 2.3×10–8 F/cm2 for 150-nm SiO2). We found that the resistivity ρ r was negligible in comparison with the first term in (1). The estimated field-effect mobility of holes in the sample of Fig. 4 is 2700cm2/Vs at room temperature and 1600cm2/Vs at T=80K. Almost the same mobility values were obtained with the use of the simple formula μ = Δ
I
ds
/ (
C
g
( W / L )
V
ds
Δ
V
g
) , where W and L are respectively the width and the length of FLG. The electron mobility is essentially lower. In pristine (not-treated in HF:H2O) samples the carrier mobility had similar values (about 3100cm2/Vs at room temperature). The background conductivity σ bg of pristine samples was 0.6mS. After a 4-min treatment, the conductivity σ bg becomes 3×10−7 mS, leading to a strong modulation of the current by gate voltage. The relatively high mobility and the possibility to easily control the film conductivity make such films a promising material for future graphene-based electronics.

To control the graphene functionalization with HF, we have identified a new treatment capable of preventing graphene samples from their modification of in HF:H2O: this is the treatment in isopropyl alcohol (IPA) to be given to graphene samples before their functionalization in HF:H2O. Resistivity data for a sample preliminarily treated for 20min in IPA and then subjected to HF treatment are shown in Fig. 3. Here, no increase in the resistivity value of the sample was observed. Moreover, the preliminary treatment during 20min in isopropyl alcohol was found to suppress the changes in the AFM images and Raman spectra during subsequent treatment of IPA-treated samples in HF aqueous solutions.

For comparison, several graphene samples were subjected to a treatment in HF vapor. The treatment duration was ranged from 1min up to 17h. In the case of small-sized flakes provided with contacts, treatment in HF vapor makes the flakes suspended over the substrate. Electrical measurements have yielded a conductivity value remaining unchanged during 17h of the treatment. So, after treatment of our samples in HF vapor no step-like increase in resistivity was observed. We would like to mention here that such samples showed no changes in their Raman spectra either.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of HF and IPA with graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Lets us discuss now additional arguments in favor of our suggestion about a fluorination reaction proceeding in graphene or FLG films on SiO2/Si surface immersed in an aqueous solution of HF. First of all, we would like to emphasize here that graphene functionalization with HF was observed only for a very narrow range of HF solutions in water (near 5%). The possibility of fluorination reaction in HF aqueous solutions for graphene oxide flakes at elevated temperatures (∼150 oC) and pressures was demonstrated by Z. Wang et al. [20]. Another mode of graphene fluorination in HF aqueous solutions was observed by M. Bruna et al. [21], who observed formation of graphene oxyfluoride in 50% solution of HF in water in an electrochemical process. For graphene, the energy cost of the first step of chemisorption for the case of HF (chemisorption of F) is known to be rather high and further decreasing with increase of the F number [16]. Simultaneously, it is a well-known fact that fluorine more readily interacts with graphene edges and with defects in graphene [13]. Our graphene or few-layer graphene exfoliated from graphite exists, on a large scale, in polycrystalline form [22]. We attribute the network surface morphology illustrated by Fig. 2 b to the formation of grain boundaries in polycrystalline graphene. So, at the first stage of functionalization in HF:H2O a network of fluorographene was assumed to nucleate at grain boundaries in our samples. Local heating due to the etching of the SiO2 substrate, and also the involvement of an aqueous solution, capable of strong modification of reaction constants, are additional important factors for reaction stimulation. As a result, after the first step a second step of the reaction for carbon atoms located near graphene edges and in a vicinity of defects in graphene becomes realizable. Moreover, the enhancement of chemical activity in corrugated graphene was predicted in [23]. An increase in HF-treatment duration leads to an increase in the reacted area of the surface, finally ending in complete transformation of the whole structure into fluorographene.

The lattice constant d of graphene is ≈2.46 A. According to [8], fluorographene has a unit cell approximately 1% larger than that of graphene, that is, d ≈2.48 A. An increase in d is expected because fluorination leads to sp3-type bonding featuring to a larger interatomic distance in comparison with sp2-bonding. However, the observed increase is small in comparison with fluorographite, where d was reported to be 2.8 to 4.5% larger than in graphite [24,25]. The smaller lattice constant d in FG is probably due to the possibility for 2D FG sheets to undergo corrugation if out-of-plane displacements of carbon atoms in FG are not restricted by the surrounding 3D matrix. For few-layer graphene, we have an intermediate situation. Local fluorination leads to the production of local mechanical stain, which leads to corrugation of the whole FLG layer. Corrugation of HF-treated samples allows us to assume that functionalization of the FLG structure with HF initially starts with the top graphene layer. With due regard for the above, it can be expected that fluorination of the top graphene layer will lead to the formation of a strained top graphene monolayer weakly connected with lower-lying graphene underlayers. Corrugation of the top layer leads to penetration of HF:H2O solution into the interlayer space and layer-by-layer functionalization of the whole sample. In view of the weak interaction of graphene with the SiO2/Si substrate, a similar process can also be expected to proceed at the graphene/SiO2 interface. Heating of our samples due to the etching of SiO2 in HF:H2O also serves a powerful stimulation for the reaction.

The most widespread recognized mechanism underlying the physical or chemical adsorption of organic molecules on the graphene surface includes the interaction of absorbates with “special” carbons, i.e. with carbon atoms localized at graphene edges, grain boundaries, and defects [26]. In case of polar molecules (such as IPA), the interaction strengthens due to the electron-donor–acceptor interaction, often leading to a transfer of charge to graphene. Hence, it can be hypothesized that adsorption of IPA on graphene surface blocks the approaching march of F ions to graphene edges, grain boundaries and other various defects, thus suppressing the fluorination of graphene in HF aqueous solution. The nature of the adsorption (chemical or physical) is not clear yet, but the estimated value of the binding energy is about 3eV.
Local treatment in IPA, in combination with graphene or FLG functionalization in HF:H2O, gives one an approach to nanostructuring of FLG and design of graphene-based devices.

Treatments in solutions of HF in water were found to result in dramatic changes of structural and electrical properties of graphene and few-layer (thickness less than 15nm) graphene flakes; those changes include an increase in resistivity (≥1011 Ω/□), vanishing of all peaks from the Raman spectra of the samples, and the formation of a periodic nanoswell relief on the surface of the flakes (step about 100nm). Reversible behavior of HF- functionalized samples due to additional anneals at 400–450°C was demonstrated. Graphene fluorination is assumed to be due to HF treatment. A network morphology was revealed on the surface of graphene flakes for samples treated in an aqueous solution of HF for times 1 to 5min ( ∼1min for graphene and ∼5min for FLG with thickness 5nm). Interaction of F atoms with grain boundaries in graphene or FLG is assumed in this case. Such a material HF-treated for a relatively short time (before the occurrence of the step-like increase in resistivity) was found to be obtainable at an early stage of the functionalization process. This material exhibits a combination of high conductivity, high carrier mobility, and the possibility of achieving a strong modulation (up to 4 orders of magnitude) of the current by gate voltage, very promising for electronic applications. A periodic nanoswell relief (step ∼80–100nm and height ∼2–6nm depending on treatment duration) was identified on the surface of graphene films treated with solutions of HF in water for longer times (more than 2min for graphene and more than 6min for 5-nm thick FLG). Formation of graphene quantum dots was assumed to occur after the step-like increase in film resistance. It was found that functionalization of graphene with HF can be controlled by a preliminary treatment of graphene in isopropyl alcohol. The latter treatment suppresses the effects due to subsequent functionalization of graphene or FLG in HF-containing solutions. The combination of the two treatments gives one a key to nanodesign of graphene-based devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was partially supported by the Russian Foundation for Basic Research (grants nos. 12–02–01275 and 11–02–00722) and by the Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation (project no. 8028).
